textPlain: Partly for reasons mentioned in the article, I use fossil for all my personal hobby projects. I still use git at work, for my old hobby projects (no sense bothering to convert all existing projects) and for exchanging code with others, but for just my solo projects I find fossil a lot simpler and easier and less distracting.Markdown is fine, especially some dialects. I try to mostly use Pandoc's Markdown. It handles new-lines in a sane way, for one thing. When I just need something simple I use gemtext (the line-based markup language used together with the Gemini protocol) (trivial to losslessly convert to Markdown, so there is no downside to start simple and switch later if necessary). For more complex things I use ORG-mode format, to get better tooling (i.e. ORG-mode itself).JSON is usually good enough to not have to consider alternatives, especially since the popular alternatives seem sadly over-engineered. Trailing commas and comments would be nice, but others would probably like other things, and when you start adding everything that is somewhat popular you get some over-engineered bloated mess, like seen in so much software. Often the right thing to do is to just accept that there are limitations and learn to live with those to avoid the otherwise seemingly inevitable feature creep.
The drive by shooting of Gruber in this article is sort of weird?!

>Why not bold and _italics_. That would make a lot more sense to me.Surely _underline_ would make more sense than _italics_. Somewhere I have seen /italics/ in use, but that does look kind of regexpy.>I dislike that trailing commas are not allowed...There is no need for this and it makes writing out valid JSON more complex.Trailing commas as a trend emerged after JSON was standardised. And thank god JSON is as well and truly standard as it is.
Coming from PHP, the lack of trailing commas in JSON always bites me.  It's annoying when I rearrange the items and now the missing comma line was buried somewhere upwards.
For the mentioned problem of removing sensitive data, BFG Repo-Cleaner does the trick: https://rtyley.github.io/bfg-repo-cleaner/
To those who may not know, Grumpy Gamer is a blog by Ron Gilbert, the creator of iconic game titles such as Maniac Mansion and Monkey Island. A true legend.

> Why is italics italics* and bold is *bold*? Why not bold and _italics_. That would make a lot more sense to me.*I think that comes from separating content and style, indicating meaning rather than explicit style: it isn't really one asterisk for italic and two for bold, it is one for emphasis and two for strong emphasis and the renderer choses how to display those levels. Like using HTML's “em” and “strong” tags instead of explicit “i” and “b” tags.
I was surprised by the comment about John Gruber; I love reading his Daring Fireball. I'll try to look at the articles from Gilbert's perspective now.
Neat little blog post. But it kept me waiting for a punchline which never appeared.

> I also have issue with it’s creator, John Gruber. He is a highly annoying smug Apple Fanboy. His writing was fine in the early days when Apple was #3, but got intolerable as Apple became the 800lb gorilla. It’s changed recently as Apple has snubbed him but I still can’t read anything he writes.Thank you! I thought it was just me...
The ad hominem is unnecessary when there are more substantial criticisms of his writing and interviews.

It’s an old convention that underline in a manuscript (handwritten or typewritten) directs the typesetter to use italics (as underlines are basically nonexistent in professional typesetting before the WWW). I expect that this is where the _italics_ thing (which predates Markdown) comes from. (There is precedent for /italics/ and I don’t think it’s unreasonable, but it is much rarer.)
The trailing comma trend is almost entirely down to line-based diff tools as far as I can tell.

Underscore for italics probably has its origins in the use of a solid underline as markup in a manuscript/typescript instructing the typesetter to set that fragment in italics (underscore generally being frowned upon in professionally set material otherwise, I think).
Trailing commas is the source of 95% of my SQL syntax errors. I know I _could_ put them at the start of the line, but it's unintuitive.
Back on Fidonet/Aminet/Spot on the Amiga in the 1990s, pre-internet, the convention was /italic/, *bold* and _underline_. This was much more intuitive for me than what we have today.
I agree with you.An article like this is weakened by including an unnecessary personal attack.
Hasn’t is always been an open secret that daring fireball it a shadow marketing website for apple?
